pages.title: Meet the Vaquita: Discovery & Unique Identity
pages.teaser: Uncover the story of the vaquita, *Phocoena sinus*, a rare porpoise first described in 1958. Learn what makes this elusive marine mammal distinct from its closest relatives.

key_terms.definition: The scientific name for the vaquita, a species of porpoise endemic to the Gulf of California.
The specific geographic location where the first specimen of a new species was collected.
A description of the distinguishing characteristics of a species, used to differentiate it from other closely related species.

pages.title: The Vaquita's Formal Introduction
pages.caption: The vaquita, or *Phocoena sinus*, was formally described by Norris and McFarland in 1958. Its 'type locality' – the place where the first specimen was found – is the northeast shore of Punto San Felipe, Baja California Norte, within Mexico's Gulf of California. This initial discovery marked the beginning of our scientific understanding of this unique species.

pages.title: A Distinct Member of the Porpoise Family
pages.caption: Classified within the Order Cetacea (whales, dolphins, and porpoises), Suborder Odontoceti (toothed whales), and Family Phocoenidae (true porpoises), *Phocoena sinus* is one of four species in its genus. Unlike some other marine mammals, no subspecies are recognized for the vaquita, emphasizing its singular identity within its limited range.

pages.title: Cranial Clues: What Sets the Vaquita Apart
pages.caption: Scientists distinguish *P. sinus* from its close relative, *P. phocoena*, by several key cranial features. The vaquita possesses a smaller cranium with a broader, shorter rostrum, a greater downward deflection of the basicranial axis, and a larger foramen magnum. These subtle yet significant differences in skull morphology are crucial for accurate species identification.

pages.title: External Distinctions: Beyond the Skull
pages.caption: Beyond skeletal differences, *P. sinus* (Vaquita) also exhibits distinct external characteristics. It is generally smaller in total length than *P. phocoena* (Harbor Porpoise), with proportionately larger flippers that have a more concave posterior border. Its dorsal fin is also proportionately higher with a more concave posterior edge. These features contribute to its unique appearance and help differentiate it in the wild.

pages.title: Vaquita Anatomy & Exclusive Habitat
pages.teaser: Dive deeper into the physical characteristics of the vaquita, from its skull to its skeleton, and explore its incredibly restricted natural habitat within the Gulf of California.

key_terms.definition: A standard measurement of skull length, from the anterior tip of the premaxillae to the posterior-most point of the occipital condyles.
The arrangement or condition of the teeth in a particular species or individual.
Native or restricted to a certain country or area.

pages.title: Measuring the Vaquita: External Dimensions
pages.caption: External measurements from two specimens reveal the vaquita's typical size. Total length ranges from 1,390 to 1,500 mm, with flippers measuring 270-280 mm from anterior insertion to tip. The dorsal fin height is 115-155 mm, and fluke width is consistently around 370 mm. These proportions suggest a closer resemblance to *P. spinipinnis* than to *P. phocoena*.

pages.title: Internal Structure: Vertebrae and Dentition
pages.caption: Internally, the vaquita possesses a unique vertebral count: 7 cervical, 12-13 thoracic, 13-15 lumbar, and 29-30 caudal vertebrae, with 19-20 chevron bones. This count is notably lower than other *Phocoena* species. Its dentition consists of 18-22 upper teeth and 17-20 lower teeth on each side, all characterized by distinctive spade-shaped crowns, uniform throughout the genus.

pages.title: The Vaquita's Exclusive Home: Upper Gulf of California
pages.caption: The vaquita's distribution is remarkably restricted, primarily to the upper Gulf of California, Mexico. While historical reports suggested a wider range, confirmed records, as shown in Figure 4, firmly place this species within this specific region. This limited habitat makes the vaquita particularly vulnerable to localized threats and environmental changes.

pages.title: Vaquita Life: Ecology, Threats & Conservation Crisis
pages.teaser: Explore the vaquita's diet, reproductive patterns, and the devastating impact of human activities, particularly bycatch in fishing nets, that have pushed this species to the brink of extinction.

key_terms.definition: The incidental capture of non-target species in fishing gear, a major threat to marine wildlife.
A large fish (*Cynoscion macdonaldi*) endemic to the Gulf of California, whose illegal fishing nets are a primary cause of vaquita bycatch.
Conservation statuses indicating a high risk of extinction in the wild, as classified by organizations like the IUCN.

pages.title: What's on the Vaquita Menu?
pages.caption: Our understanding of the vaquita's diet comes from stomach content analysis. One specimen was found to have otoliths (ear bones) from bronze striped grunts (*Orthopristis reddingi*) and gulf croakers (*Bairdiella icistius*), both abundant fish in the northern Gulf of California. Remains of squid were also present, indicating a varied diet of locally available prey.

pages.title: Glimpses of Reproduction: Mother and Calf
pages.caption: Little is known about vaquita reproduction, but a poignant photograph (Figure 5) captures a probable female/calf pair tragically caught in a gill net. The calf, estimated at 74 cm, shows visible fetal folds, suggesting it was very young. This image underscores the species' vulnerability and the challenges in studying its life history.

pages.title: The Silent Killer: Gill Net Bycatch
pages.caption: The primary threat to vaquitas is incidental capture, or bycatch, in gill nets. Historically, these nets were used for fishing totoaba (*Cynoscion macdonaldi*), a large fish also endemic to the Gulf. Even after a ban on totoaba fishing in 1975, gill net use for other species and illegal totoaba fishing continued, leading to devastating vaquita mortality. Early 1970s estimates suggested annual bycatch in the tens to hundreds.

pages.title: A Species on the Brink: Conservation Status
pages.caption: The vaquita's precarious situation has led to its listing as 'Vulnerable' by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) in 1978, and later in Appendix I of the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species (CITES) in 1979. These designations highlight the urgent need for conservation efforts to protect this critically endangered species from extinction, a direct consequence of human fishing practices.
